NOTICE OF WITHDRAWAL OF MOTION COMPELLING THIRD PARTY SN SERVICING CORPORATION TO: (1)PRODUCE DOCUMENTS PURSUANT TO A SUBPOENA; (2)MONETARY SANCTIONS; AND (3)ISSUANCE OF AN ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE RE: CONTEMPT (TRANSACTION ID # 200021010) FILED BY DEFENDANT SETERUS, INC.
MINI MINUTES FOR DEC-11-2020 09:00 AM FOR DEPT 302

DISCOVERY, DEPT. 302, AS TO THE DEC-11-2020 HEARING RE: DEFENDANT SETERUS, INC.'S MOTION COMPELLING THIRD PARTY SN SERVICING CORPORATION TO: (1) PRODUCE DOCUMENTS, PURSUANT TO A SUBPOENA; (2) MONETARY SANCTIONS; AND (3) ISSUANCE OF AN ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE RE: CONTEMPT, THE COURT'S TENTATIVE RULING IS GRANTED. THE MOTION IS GRANTED. NO OPPOSITION FILED. THE MOVING PARTY'S REQUEST FOR MONETARY SANCTIONS IS GRANTED IN THE AMOUNT OF $1,600, PAYABLE WITHIN 14 DAYS OF NOTICE OF ENTRY OF ORDER. THE MOVING PARTY'S REQUEST FOR THE ISSUANCE OF AN ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE IS DENIED. THE PREVAILING PARTY IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE AND SUBMIT A PROPOSED ORDER TO THE JUDGE PRO TEM AT NOAH@NDLLEGAL.COM. JUDGE PRO TEM: NOAH LEBOWITZ; CLERK: SEAN KANE; NOT REPORTED. (302/JPT)

Notice Of Motions And Motions To Amend, File Supplemental Complaint, And For Reclassification; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Of Motions
Off Calendar Feb-18-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFFs TIMOTHY BOSTWICK and MICHELE NESSIER's Motions To Amend, File Supplemental Complaint, And For Reclassification:

Plaintiffs' motion to file a second amended and first supplemental complaint is granted. Defendant Seterus, Inc. opposes the motion only to the extent that Plaintiffs seek to name it in a new cause of action for violation of Civil Code section 2924c. Seterus may challenge the viability of that new cause of action by demurrer or other appropriate motion. (See, e.g., California Casualty Gen. Ins. v. Superior Court (1985) 173 Cal.App.3d 274, 281 ["the preferable practice would be to permit the amendment and allow the parties to test its legal sufficiency by demurrer, motion for judgment on the pleadings or other appropriate proceedings."].) Plaintiffs' motion for reclassification is granted as unopposed.

Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)

ARBITRATION ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Oct-06-2020 Continued To Feb-23-21 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
Off Calendar Jun-29-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Hearing Re: Defendant Seterus, Inc.'s Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings.

No appearances made by the parties. The Court adopts its tentative ruling:

Defendant's motion for judgment on the pleadings is denied. Defendant fails to show that the mortgage debt at issue in the complaint does not fall within the Rosenthal Fair Debt Collection Practices Act ("Rosenthal Act").

The Rosenthal Act defines "consumer debt" to "mean money, property, or their equivalent, due or owing or alleged to be due or owing from a natural person by reason of a consumer credit transaction. The term 'consumer debt' includes a mortgage debt." (Civ. Code 1788.2(f).) Defendant omits to quote the final sentence, which was added in 2020 to clarify that for purposes of the Act, mortgage debt is included.

Plaintiffs allege that the mortgage loan in question was made to them primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. (FAC 11.) Defendant does not cite any authority supporting its contention that "mortgage debt" as used in the Rosenthal Act is limited to debt on an individual's principal residence. Contrary to defendant's argument in its reply papers, that point is not a "straw man": defendant explicitly argued in its moving papers that the Rosenthal Act applies to loans secured by real property "only when the property is the borrower's principal residence." (Mot. at 5:25-26; see also id. at 3:19-20 [arguing that the loan "is not for Plaintiffs' principal residence but a second home that they lease to a third party."]; id. at 6:7-8 [citing district court decision holding that FDCPA did not apply because "'Plaintiffs did not use the property as a primary residence'"].)

Remarkably, although the Court previously criticized defendant for failing to address closely analogous (if not binding) California authority in its moving papers on demurrer, defendant commits the same error again, and on reply asserts that the case in question is "irrelevant." Wrong. Contrary to defendant's position, the Rosenthal Act applies to mortgage debt, whether it is on a primary or a secondary residence. (Davidson v. Seterus, Inc. (2018) 21 Cal.App.5th 283, 296 ["It is readily apparent that persons who obtain mortgages very often do so in order to purchase a primary or even secondary residence, which may be considered to be a 'personal, family, or household purpose[].' ( 1788.2, subd. (e).)"].) Defendant's position is based entirely on some lower federal courts' restrictive interpretation of the Federal Fair Debt Collection Practices Act ("FDCPA"). (Cf. Gilliam, Trustee of Lou Easter Ross Revocable Trust v. Levine, Trustee of Joel Sherman Revocable Trust (9th Cir. 2020) 955 F.3d 1117, 1121-1122 [rejecting contention that a residential loan to a trust can be considered a consumer credit transaction only where the trustee-borrower lives at the residence]; Miller v. McCalla, Raymer, Padrick, Cobb, Nichols, and Clark, L.L.C. (7th Cir. 2000) 214 F.3d 872, 874-875 [holding that FDCPA applied to mortgage on property that plaintiff purchased in 1992 and rented out beginning in 1995; "the relevant time is when the loan is made, not when collection is attempted"].) However, the Rosenthal Act is not identical to the FDCPA. "The Rosenthal Act does not mirror the FDCPA?." (Davidson, 21 Cal.App.4th at 303.) Further, as a civil statute that was enacted for the protection of the public, the Rosenthal Act must be construed broadly in favor of protecting the public. (Id. at 289.) Finally, that plaintiffs may have rented out their secondary residence to obtain rental income is not necessarily inconsistent with the mortgage loan having been made primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, and cannot entitle defendant to judgment on the pleadings as a matter of law.

Plaintiff to prepare and submit a proposed order.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/EPS)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar May-08-2019 Continued To Jun-26-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-25-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Duncan Nefcy, Esq, Mellen Lasw firm, 510-263-9638, appearing for Plaintiff(s); CourtCall)
Michael W. Stoltzman Jr., The Ryan Firm, 949-263-1800, appearing for Defendant Seterus, Inc.; (CourtCall)

RULING - A R G U E D. Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Seterus, Inc.'s demurrer to the first amended complaint.

Defendant Seterus, Inc.'s demurrer to the first amended complaint is overruled. In Davidson v. Seterus, Inc. (2018) 21 Cal.App.5th 283, which Defendant failed to address in its moving papers, the court reversed the trial court's order sustaining the defendants' demurrer, holding squarely that "the Rosenthal Act's definition of 'debt collector' applies to a mortgage servicer who engages in debt collection practices in attempting to obtain repayment of mortgage debt." (Id. at 304; see also id. at 303 ["In our view, those federal courts that have concluded that section 1788.2 's definition of 'debt collector' may include mortgage lenders and mortgage servicers have the better position under an analysis of the actual statutory language."].)

Defendant also argues, relying upon Obduskey v. McCarthy & Holthus LLP (2019) 139 S.Ct. 1029, that its December 17, 2018 letter to Plaintiffs (FAC Paras. 13, 15, 18) did not qualify as "debt collection." Obduskey does not bar Plaintiffs' claim. In Obduskey, the Supreme Court held only that an entity whose principal purpose is to enforce a security interest is not a "debt collector" under the federal Fair Debt Collection Practices Act. The Court explained that a law firm could not be liable for its conduct as part of the nonjudicial foreclosure process. In this case, Defendant's communications were not principally to enforce a security interest. Both the 12/12 and 12/17 letters were about making payments and "collecting" on the loan; the foreclosure process had yet to commence. Moreover, the definitions of "debt collector" under the Rosenthal Act and the FDCPA are different. (See Davidson v. Seterus, 21 Cal.App.5th at 303-304.) Defendant's reliance on Civil Code Sec. 2923.6 does not change the analysis. First, it is unclear whether the 30-day appeal notice was really a step antecedent to enforcing a security interest. Second, the conflicting language in the letters about collection clearly implicate the Act. Lastly, Civil Code Sec. 2924(b) shows that only the trustee on the deed of trust (and not a mortgage servicer such as Defendant) is immune from liability under the Rosenthal Act.

Plaintiffs sufficiently pleads damages. Paragraph 28 alleges emotional distress damages, which are recoverable under the Rosenthal Act. (Civ. Code Sec. 1788.30(b) [debtor may recover actual damages for willful and knowing violation]; Komarova v. National Credit Acceptance, Inc. (2009) 175 Cal.App.4th 324, 341 n.3 ["It is not disputed . . . that 'actual damages' recoverable under the Act (Sec. 1788.30) include those for the plaintiff's emotional distress."]. Plaintiffs can also recover a penalty under Civil Code Sec. 1788.30(b).

Order is signed

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Rebecca Tovar CSR#14200, email: rtovar@sftc.org, Reported. =(302/EPS)

MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-25-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Duncan Nefcy, Esq, Mellen Lasw firm, 510-263-9638, appearing for Plaintiff(s); CourtCall)
Michael W. Stoltzman Jr., The Ryan Firm, 949-263-1800, appearing for Defendant Seterus, Inc.; (CourtCall)

RULING - A R G U E D. Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT SETERUS, INC.'S MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Defendant Seterus, Inc.'s motion to strike the claim for punitive damages is granted without leave to amend. First, plaintiff does not allege any facts showing "despicable" conduct. (See Mock v. Michigan Millers Mutual Ins. Co. (1992) 4 Cal.App.4th 306, 331.) Second, most cases that have addressed whether punitive damages are recoverable on a Rosenthal Act claim have ruled that they are not. (See Komarova v. National Credit Acceptance, Inc. (2009) 175 Cal.App.4th 324, 341 n.3 ["It is not disputed that punitive damages are unavailable under the Act . . . Precluding causes of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress in Rosenthal Act cases thus serves only to eliminate recovery of punitive damages."].)

Order is signed

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Rebecca Tovar CSR#14200, email: rtovar@sftc.org, Reported. =(302/EPS)
